steps.text: Obtain guidance, as appropriate, and clearance from the Office of the Solicitor.

Obtain input, technical or otherwise, and policy clearance of proposed issuances/directives from each program director, free-standing office, regional administrator, and others identified as mandatory clearance offices.

Refer to the appropriate Deputy Assistant Secretary for any unsolved disagreements or issues concerning the substance or potential policy implications of the proposed policy issuance or the priority according to its review that emerged during the clearance process.

Consider each proposed issuance and promptly respond to the originator, regarding technical considerations, potential impact of the programs and policies, implications of Agency-wide policy and operations, and anticipated reception by OSHA staff, partners, public, and other interested parties.
Administer the OSHA directive program.
Ensure compliance with directives and related instructions.
Provide advice, assistance, and training to national office directorates and regional offices in directives management, including the special requirements of the revised system.
Provide new or revised directive documents to the web administrator for posting to the directives webpage.
Periodically notify the web administrator of updates to the webpage based on cancellations and expired directives.
Provide unsigned 508 compliant PDF file versions to directives officer for posting.
Update necessary directorate webpages to reflect new directives.
Disseminate directives as appropriate.
Review and respond to draft directives received from national office directorates.
Implement OSHA national directives throughout the region.
Clear and publish regional directives supplementing OSHA national directives.
Provide 508-compliant PDF file versions to the directives officer for posting.
Update necessary regional webpages to reflect new directives.
Disseminate regional instructions as necessary.
Distribute new and updated directives to all employees.
Specify formatting requirements for material type.
Establish clearance timeframes.
Establish the process for comment resolution.
Identify all mandatory review and clearance officials (intra- and extra-organizational).
State Programs (DCSP) must approve appropriate language for all federal program change paragraphs.
Construction-related directives must have appropriate language approved by the Director of the Directorate of Construction (DOC).
Directives affecting field operations must be transmitted and cleared by all Regional Administrators.
All directives originating in the National Office must be transmitted to SOL after concurrence from all required clearance officials.

Regional directives must be cleared by the Regional Administrator, Deputy Regional Administrator, affected Area Office Directors, National Office directorates and free-standing offices responsible for the functions covered by the directive, and the directives officer.
Regional directives must be transmitted to the appropriate Regional Office of the Solicitor after concurrence from all required clearance officials.
Originators should be selective in clearing draft directives outside OSHA.
Organizations that should be included are those responsible for programs, standards, or procedures affected by the draft directive.
Organizations that should be included are those administratively or legally responsible for reviewing the draft directive.
Chief executives of state and local governments or consultation organizations may comment on proposed directives which affect those programs.
Complete items 1 through 8 on the OSHA 201.
Determine whether field coordination is necessary.
Allow a minimum of 15 working days for review and clearance.
Prepare an electronic version of the OSHA 201 and a draft directive to provide electronically to all clearance officials and proxies.
Identify all clearance officials and proxies who will receive a copy.
Obtain the approval of their Program Director and send copies to the clearance officials and proxies.
Review and reconcile any comments noted in item 8 of the OSHA 201 by clearance officials/proxies in the National Office and in the field.
Consult with clearance officials regarding comments, proposed policy and procedure changes, and proposed language.

Contact nonresponsive clearance offices prior to the review deadline to determine whether clearance/comments will be submitted by the requested deadline and offer an opportunity to discuss any questions or issues to expedite the process.

Refer to the Program Director any unresolved disagreements between the originator and clearance officials/proxies concerning the substance of the proposed policy issuance or the priority accorded to its review that has emerged during the clearance process.
Revise the draft directive and if necessary, complete an additional clearance round for concurrence.
Provide to the Office of the Solicitor, prior to submission to the directives officer, the draft directive for review/concurrence.
Prepare directive clearance packet for processing through the directives officer requesting approval and signature of the Assistant Secretary or the Agency Administrative Officer, as appropriate.
Provide the final unsigned 508-compliant PDF file to the directives officer for posting to the directives webpage.
Consult OAS for a shorter clearance time.
Contact the clearance official to negotiate a satisfactory extension for review or meet to resolve any issues, questions, or concerns.
Schedule a meeting with the appropriate Deputy Assistant Secretary to resolve the matter.
Elevate any outstanding issues to the Assistant Secretary for a decision on whether to issue the directive.
Provide union notification in accordance with Local 12 Collective Bargaining Agreement.
Submit publication requests to the web administrator.
Ensure all final unsigned directives are 508 compliant before submission to the directives officer.
Prepare a cancellation notice using the cancellation template on the OSHA Directives Tools webpage.
Obtain the approval of their Program Director.
Provide the cancellation notice and concurrence grid providing review by the originating directorate, the directives officer, and the OAS.
Review the cancellation packet to determine if complete and accurate for the approval process.
Provide revision request from the OAS to the originator for necessary updates.
Provide notification of signed directive to the originator and request the cancellation notice be formatted to 508-compliant PDF.
Assign an effective date based on the approval by the Assistant Secretary.
Provide the final 508-compliant PDF file to the web administrator for posting to the OSHA Directives webpage.
Review 508-compliant PDF for web-related technical content.
Publish 508-compliant PDF to OSHA Directives webpage within requested.
Maintain files in accordance with OSHA file retention policies.
Include a final signed directive with directive number, signed date, effective date, and electronic signature.
Maintain all signed OSHA 201 forms and non-concurrence forms along with the supplemental concurrence form or email.
Record comments received and resolutions to those comments.
Send draft documents that have gone through the clearance process.
Provide talking points to the Assistant Secretary’s Office.
Prepare a concurrence grid.
Implement and comply with the instructions governing the management of policy and non-policy issues.
Self-assess and routinely monitor the directive process using the checklist on the directives tools webpage.
Review and assess active directives on an annual basis and maintain a final document of review findings.
Delegate authority of review and assessment, as appropriate.
Conduct a full assessment of approved directive files each year and ensure inclusion of all appropriate documents.
Send an email or memo documenting completion of requirement to the Director, Office of Audit, DAP, or designee no later than September 30.
Retain completion documentation in the directive file record.
Audit review and assessment of program findings.
Confirm the self-assessment documentation was received within the established deadline in the first quarter of each fiscal year.
Include the auditing of the assessment process as part of audit responsibilities.
